name: Out of Body Experience
description: An Out-of-Body Experience (OBE) is a phenomenon where a person feels as though their consciousness has separated from their physical body. Proponents describe this as perceiving the world from a location outside their body, sometimes reporting detailed observations of their surroundings and even traveling to distant locations. OBEs have been reported across cultures and throughout history, often linked to near-death experiences (NDEs), meditation, sleep paralysis, and drug use. Some spiritual and esoteric traditions interpret OBEs as evidence of a soul or astral body that can exist independently of the physical form. Claims include the ability to gain knowledge unavailable through ordinary senses and to interact with spiritual realms. Mainstream science generally attributes OBEs to neurological or psychological factors. Theories suggest they may be caused by disruptions in brain activity, particularly in the temporoparietal junction (TPJ), which is involved in self-perception and spatial awareness. Studies have used brain imaging techniques to explore the neural correlates of OBEs, finding correlations with altered brain activity and sensory processing. Skeptics argue that OBEs are subjective experiences resulting from altered states of consciousness or vivid imaginations, rather than objective realities. They point to the lack of verifiable evidence of information gained during OBEs that could not have been obtained through other means. Controlled experiments have been difficult to conduct due to the unpredictable nature of OBEs and the challenges of objective verification.
subtitle: Consciousness outside the physical body

claims.claim: Consciousness can separate from the physical body.
claims.evidence: Personal accounts of perceiving the world from an external viewpoint, ability to describe details of distant locations during OBEs.
claims.criticism: Subjective experiences, lack of verifiable evidence, potential for memory distortion, neurological explanations.

claims.claim: OBEs provide access to information unavailable through normal senses.
claims.evidence: Reports of accurate observations made during OBEs that were later confirmed.
claims.criticism: Possibility of prior knowledge, lucky guesses, or unintentional cues received during the experience, confirmation bias.

claims.claim: OBEs are evidence of a soul or astral body.
claims.evidence: Correlation with near-death experiences and spiritual beliefs, consistency with concepts of astral projection.
claims.criticism: Lack of scientific evidence for the existence of a soul or astral body, alternative explanations based on neurological and psychological processes.

keyFigures.name: Robert Monroe
keyFigures.role: Pioneer in OBE research and author of 'Journeys Out of the Body'

keyFigures.name: Susan Blackmore
keyFigures.role: Skeptic and researcher of OBEs, author of 'Dying to Live: Science and the Near-Death Experience'

keyFigures.name: Olaf Blanke
keyFigures.role: Neuroscientist who has studied the neural correlates of OBEs

mythologyConnections: Ancient Egyptian concepts of the Ka

timeline.event: Reports of experiences resembling OBEs in various cultures and spiritual traditions.
Emergence of Spiritualism and Theosophy, with increased interest in altered states of consciousness.
Robert Monroe's research and popularization of OBEs.
Neuroscientific research into the neural correlates of OBEs.
